one_line_takeaway: Threshold-neighboring spiking neurons are the root cause of SNN adversarial fragility, and constraining membrane potentials away from threshold plus adding firing noise (TGO) yields SOTA, inference-cost-free robustness that composes with adversarial training.
key_finding: TGO consistently reaches state-of-the-art robustness across architectures, attacks, and training strategies while adding no inference overhead. Under plain BPTT it lifts robustness by roughly 10-20% on FGSM/RFGSM, and it stacks with AT/RAT for ~10% gains under strong PGD. The authors' own analysis shows TGO reduces the number of threshold-neighboring neurons by about 40%, directly validating the theory that these neurons drive SNN adversarial vulnerability. Ablation confirms MC and NLIF are complementary (each helps alone; together they exceed the sum), and TGO still beats the SR baseline under APGD/MTPGD with EoT, indicating the gains are not merely gradient masking.
core_contribution: The paper (ICLR 2026) identifies threshold-neighboring spiking neurons as the key bottleneck for adversarial robustness in directly trained Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs), and proposes Threshold Guarding Optimization (TGO) as a defense. TGO has two synergistic components introduced by the authors: (1) a Membrane potential Constraint (MC) added to the loss that pushes neuron membrane potentials away from the firing threshold to increase gradient sparsity and lower the theoretical upper bound of gradient-based attacks; and (2) Noisy-LIF (NLIF) neurons that add Gaussian white noise to the membrane potential, converting firing from deterministic to probabilistic and reducing the state-flipping probability of the neurons that remain near the threshold. A dynamic cosine-scheduled Lagrange multiplier lambda balances clean accuracy against robustness during training. TGO adds no inference-time compute overhead and is compatible with vanilla (BPTT), Adversarial Training (AT), and Regularized AT (RAT).
threat_model: White-box, gradient-based evasion attacks against directly trained SNN image classifiers. The attacker has access to model gradients and crafts bounded perturbations (l-infinity, epsilon = 8/255) via FGSM, RFGSM, PGD (7/10/20/40 iterations), Auto-PGD (APGD), and Multi-Targeted PGD (MTPGD), including under Expectation-over-Transformation (EoT, N=3) to counter the randomness that NLIF introduces (i.e., to rule out gradient masking / obfuscated-gradient defenses). Goal is misclassification; defense is measured as retained classification accuracy under attack.
limitations: Clean accuracy drops noticeably as a robustness trade-off (e.g., CIFAR-10 WRN-16 BPTT clean 93.32 -> 88.79; the paper explicitly notes higher lambda_max lowers clean accuracy). Absolute robustness under strong multi-step attacks remains low in the vanilla setting (e.g., CIFAR-10 WRN-16 BPTT PGD40 only 0.45%), so meaningful protection still depends on combining TGO with AT/RAT. Evaluation is limited to small static-image datasets (CIFAR-10/100) and two architectures at T=4; no neuromorphic event-stream datasets (e.g., DVS) or larger-scale benchmarks are tested. NLIF introduces run-to-run variance from its stochastic firing, and the appendix/proof details are not included in the reviewed body text.

quantitative_results: CIFAR-10 / WRN-16 / BPTT: FGSM accuracy 14.05% (Vanilla) -> 51.40% (TGO); RFGSM 31.21% -> 71.38%; clean 93.32% -> 88.79%
CIFAR-100 / WRN-16 / AT: FGSM 21.18% (AT) vs 28.27% (SR) vs 41.99% (TGO); PGD7 27.78% (TGO)
Ablation (CIFAR-100 / VGG-11 / BPTT, FGSM): baseline 5.9% -> MC only 17.1% -> NLIF only 8.1% -> MC+NLIF 21.5%
TGO reduces the number of threshold-neighboring neurons by approximately 40%
MTPGD+EoT (CIFAR-100 / WRN-16, 7 steps): AT 10.01% vs SR+AT 16.88% vs TGO+AT 21.23%; APGD 7 steps: AT 9.34% vs SR+AT 14.48% vs TGO+AT 18.93%
Attack budget epsilon = 8/255 (step size 0.01); models simulated at T=4 time steps
baselines_compared: AT / HIRE-SNN input-noise adversarial training (Kundu et al., 2021)
RAT / SNN-RAT regularized adversarial training with Lipschitz penalty (Ding et al., 2022)
DLIF / robust stable SNN (Ding et al., 2024a)
SR sparse-gradient regularization (Ding et al., 2024b / Liu et al., 2024)
